root_cause: A service-mesh policy release changed checkout-path traffic to fall back cross-zone earlier and increased retry budgets. When inventory capacity in one zone was temporarily reduced for maintenance, checkout calls began traversing longer cross-zone paths and timing out more often. Mesh retries amplified that latency into duplicated reservation work, queue backpressure, and timeout propagation through pricing and checkout. Payment symptoms were secondary because requests often reached payment only after the upstream deadline was already exhausted.

details: Mesh retry and cross-zone fallback warnings begin before payment warnings and before inventory queue saturation peaks
Payment probes remain healthy while payment receives deadline-canceled traffic
mesh_sidecar.cross_zone_routing_percent and retry_rate_percent rise sharply ahead of the worst checkout failures
pricing cache remains healthy, eliminating a pricing cache miss hypothesis
Commit 92ac103 changed fallback selection earlier under local scarcity
Commit 4f1d201 increased retries on latency-sensitive checkout calls
Mesh rollout and inventory maintenance overlap before impact, and rollback reduces retries and cross-zone routing before full checkout recovery
The maintenance window created the endpoint scarcity that turned the new routing policy from harmless into pathological
checkout-orchestrator, pricing-api, inventory-api, payment-adapter, and mesh-sidecars degrade in a clear dependency chain

recommended_actions: Cap retries by end-to-end checkout budget rather than per-hop policy defaults
Disallow aggressive cross-zone fallback changes when endpoint pools are already reduced by maintenance
Alert on rising cross-zone routing plus retry overflow as an early distributed timeout-chain signal
expected_investigation_path: Resist anchoring on the last visible service in the user workflow
Build a per-service timeline to find which latencies and retries move first
Correlate mesh-policy changes with maintenance context and downstream queue buildup
Confirm the hypothesis using rollback ordering and the persistence of healthy payment and pricing cache signals
